title: Cauliflower mac and cheese
subtitle: Macaroni and cauliflower baked in a sharp cheddar sauce
excerpt: Cauliflower added to a classic macaroni cheese makes a more substantial bake with some texture to contrast the creamy sauce. The cauliflower softens slightly in the oven but holds its shape, giving each forkful both pasta and vegetable.
sourceNotes: British adaptation of the classic macaroni cheese, adding cauliflower to make the dish go further and add texture. Cauliflower cheese and macaroni cheese are both established British dishes; this combination is a common British home-cooking shortcut.

recipe.freezeNotes: Assemble and freeze unbaked for up to 2 months. Bake from frozen at 180°C for 50 minutes, foil-covered for the first 20 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble a day ahead and refrigerate, then bake as directed.

content.text: Cauliflower mac and cheese combines the two most popular British baked dishes into one tin. The cauliflower adds body and gives the sauce something to cling to beyond the pasta. A mustard-sharp cheddar sauce brings it together, and the top goes golden and slightly crunchy.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Add the cauliflower florets and cook for 3 minutes. Add the pasta and cook together for 2 minutes less than the pasta packet says. Drain.
Melt the butter in the same large pot over a medium heat. Add the flour and stir for 1 minute. Add the milk gradually, whisking to prevent lumps, and cook for 5 to 6 minutes until thickened.
Remove from the heat. Stir in three-quarters of the cheese, the mustard, and a few gratings of nutmeg. Season with salt and pepper. Fold in the drained pasta and cauliflower.
Tip into a baking tin. Scatter the remaining cheese on top. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the top is golden and bubbling. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.cause: It was boiled too long before baking.
items.fix: Boil for only 3 minutes; it will finish cooking in the oven.

items.cause: The cheese was added when the sauce was too hot, causing it to split.
items.fix: Take the sauce off the heat before adding cheese, and use mature rather than mild cheddar, which melts more reliably.

items.cause: Too low a shelf or temperature.
items.fix: Move to the top shelf and raise the grill for the last 3 minutes.

items.cause: Too much pasta and not enough sauce.
items.fix: The sauce should seem slightly loose when assembled; it thickens in the oven.

content.text: Cauliflower cheese and macaroni cheese are both British staples with long histories. Combining them into one tin is a practical piece of home-kitchen economy: two dishes for the effort of one, a more substantial result, and nothing wasted. It is firmly in the British tradition of stretching a cheese sauce as far as it will go.
